threats.title: onlyOperator Modifier Inverted Logic Locks Out All Designated Operators
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyOperator modifier uses `if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert` which reverts when operator[msg.sender] is true. This means designated operators can NEVER call harvest, delegateAmount, undelegateAmount, redelegateAmount, harvestAndDelegateRewards, or finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. Additionally, if the owner is also set as an operator via updateOperator(owner, true), even the owner is permanently locked out of all operator functions until the mapping is toggled back.
threats.investigation_rationale: The boolean condition `msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]` evaluates to true (reverts) whenever operator[msg.sender] is true, regardless of ownership. The intended logic is clearly `msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]` — allowing either the owner OR an operator to proceed. This is a critical access control defect affecting all staking management functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert — OR operator should use AND with negation: != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]

preconditions: An address is set as operator via updateOperator(addr, true)
That operator attempts to call any onlyOperator-guarded function

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect boolean logic in access control modifier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: operator[msg.sender] being true causes revert instead of allowing access
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No test coverage for operator-as-non-owner calling gated functions

analysis_questions: Does the deployment script set any operators? (No — only owner deploys and calls directly)
Can the owner still call operator functions? (Yes, only if operator[owner] is false)
If owner calls updateOperator(owner, true), are all operator functions bricked?
validation_requirements: Test: set operator[addr]=true, have addr call harvest() — expect revert
Test: set operator[owner]=true, have owner call delegateAmount() — expect revert
Verify no alternate code path bypasses the modifier

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After deployment, if the owner sets a bot as operator and assumes the bot handles harvesting/finalization, all staking management stops. User withdrawal requests via requestWithdrawal cannot be finalized. The owner must directly manage all operations without being flagged as operator.
prerequisites: Owner sets at least one operator address via updateOperator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total protocol paralysis if owner is also set as operator; delegation of staking ops impossible otherwise
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users — staking cannot be managed by designated operators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can toggle operator[owner]=false to regain access, but designated operators remain permanently locked out

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that non-owner operators can call operator-gated functions

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Immediate on any operator function call

threats.title: harvestAndDelegateRewards Creates Phantom Fee Debt That Drains User Deposits
threats.hypothesis_description: When harvestAndDelegateRewards is called, rewards are restaked directly via delegateRewards (no RON/WRON enters the vault), but operatorFeeAmount is incremented as if the rewards entered the vault. When fetchOperatorFee is called, it withdraws WRON from the vault balance — WRON that belongs to depositors, not to the operator. This creates a solvency drain proportional to accumulated harvestAndDelegateRewards fee amounts.
threats.investigation_rationale: In LiquidProxy.harvestAndDelegateRewards, the staking contract restakes rewards directly. The returned claimableAmount is passed back to LiquidRon, which adds (claimableAmount * operatorFee / BIPS) to operatorFeeAmount. But no WRON was deposited into the vault. fetchOperatorFee then calls _withdrawRONTo(feeRecipient, amount) using vault WRON that belongs to depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: operatorFeeAmount += (harvestedAmount * operatorFee) / BIPS but harvestedAmount comes from delegateRewards (no WRON deposited to vault)

preconditions: Owner calls harvestAndDelegateRewards (operator functions only callable by non-operator owner)
Rewards exist to be restaked
feeRecipient calls fetchOperatorFee

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Fee accounting on rewards that never enter vault
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: LiquidProxy.harvestAndDelegateRewards returns claimableAmount from delegateRewards, vault adds fee on it without receiving corresponding WRON
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that vault WRON balance increased before incrementing operatorFeeAmount

analysis_questions: How much WRON enters the vault during harvestAndDelegateRewards? (Zero — rewards are restaked)
Is operatorFeeAmount ever bounded by vault balance?
Could repeated calls make operatorFeeAmount exceed total vault WRON?
validation_requirements: Trace WRON flow: confirm no WRON enters vault during harvestAndDelegateRewards
Simulate: harvestAndDelegateRewards with large rewards, then fetchOperatorFee, check vault solvency
Calculate cumulative drain over multiple cycles

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Normal protocol operation: owner calls harvestAndDelegateRewards to compound rewards. operatorFeeAmount grows with each call. feeRecipient calls fetchOperatorFee, draining depositor WRON. Over time, vault becomes insolvent relative to share liabilities.
prerequisites: harvestAndDelegateRewards called at least once with non-zero rewards

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Direct drain of depositor funds equal to sum of (restaked_rewards * operatorFee / BIPS) across all harvestAndDelegateRewards calls
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors — their principal is drained
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — WRON already withdrawn by feeRecipient

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying vault solvency after harvestAndDelegateRewards + fetchOperatorFee

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Vault WRON balance decreasing without corresponding share burns

threats.title: totalAssets Inflated by operatorFeeAmount Causing Share Price Distortion
threats.hypothesis_description: After harvest() is called, full harvested WRON enters the vault balance and is counted in super.totalAssets(). The operatorFeeAmount (portion belonging to fee recipient) is tracked separately but NOT subtracted from totalAssets(). This inflates the share price by the fee amount. When fetchOperatorFee is called, totalAssets drops by the fee amount, causing a share price decrease that harms existing holders. The asymmetry creates a predictable share price fluctuation around harvest/fee-claim cycles.
threats.investigation_rationale: totalAssets() = super.totalAssets() + getTotalStaked() + getTotalRewards(). After harvest, getTotalRewards drops to ~0, super.totalAssets() increases by full harvest amount (including fee portion). The fee portion should be excluded since it belongs to feeRecipient, not shareholders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalAssets() = super.totalAssets() + getTotalStaked() + getTotalRewards() — missing subtraction of operatorFeeAmount

preconditions: harvest() has been called at least once with operatorFee > 0
operatorFeeAmount > 0 (fees accumulated but not yet claimed)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalAssets includes operatorFeeAmount which belongs to fee recipient
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Share price = totalAssets / totalSupply is inflated by fee amount
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No subtraction of operatorFeeAmount in totalAssets

analysis_questions: What is the maximum operatorFeeAmount relative to totalAssets?
How does the fee inflation affect deposit/withdrawal share calculations?
Can depositors after harvest but before fee claim extract excess value?
validation_requirements: Calculate totalAssets before and after harvest — confirm inflation by fee amount
Calculate totalAssets before and after fetchOperatorFee — confirm deflation
Quantify share price distortion as a percentage

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After a large harvest, totalAssets is inflated by operatorFeeAmount. New depositors get fewer shares than fair value. When feeRecipient claims fees, totalAssets drops and these depositors suffer a loss. Existing depositors who redeem between harvest and fee claim extract excess value.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Share price distortion proportional to operatorFeeAmount / totalAssets — up to ~2.5% of harvested rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors and withdrawers during the inflation window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Loss is permanent once withdrawn at inflated/deflated price

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying totalAssets excludes operatorFeeAmount

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Depends on pool size and harvest amount
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable on-chain via totalAssets changes around harvest events

threats.title: LiquidProxy.harvest() Claims Rewards N Times Redundantly, Risking DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.harvest() loops over _consensusAddrs.length and calls claimRewards(_consensusAddrs) — passing the FULL array — on EACH iteration. This means rewards are claimed N times for the same set of validators. The first call claims all rewards; subsequent calls attempt to claim zero. If the Ronin staking contract reverts on zero-reward claims, harvest becomes impossible for arrays with length > 1. Additionally, the function uses address(this).balance as claimed amount, which includes any pre-existing native RON from prior operations or external sends to the proxy's open receive().
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop iterates `_consensusAddrs.length` times, but each iteration passes the entire `_consensusAddrs` array to claimRewards, not just `_consensusAddrs[i]`. This is a clear code defect — the intent was likely to call once or to call per-address.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (i = 0; i < _consensusAddrs.length; i++) { claimRewards(_consensusAddrs); } — passes full array in loop

preconditions: _consensusAddrs.length > 1
Ronin staking contract behavior on duplicate/zero-reward claims must be verified

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Full array passed in loop instead of single element or single call
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: N redundant claimRewards calls; also address(this).balance includes pre-existing RON
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance delta tracking; no single-call pattern

analysis_questions: Does Ronin staking contract revert on claimRewards with zero claimable rewards?
If so, harvest is permanently DoS'd for multi-validator arrays
How much gas is wasted on redundant calls?
validation_requirements: Test claimRewards behavior with zero rewards (does it revert?)
Measure gas cost with multiple validators
Test harvest with pre-existing RON in proxy via open receive()

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Ronin staking reverts on zero-reward claims, any harvest call with >1 consensus address fails after the first iteration claims everything. This blocks all reward collection. Even if it doesn't revert, N-1 redundant calls waste gas and the balance-based accounting is inaccurate.
prerequisites: Multiple consensus addresses passed to harvest
Ronin staking contract behavior on duplicate claims

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete reward harvesting failure if staking contract reverts on empty claims; otherwise gas waste and accounting inaccuracy
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users — rewards cannot be harvested
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Workaround: call harvest with single-element arrays (wastes gas)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for multi-validator harvest behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable via failed harvest transactions

threats.title: No Withdrawal Request Cancellation Creates Permanent Fund Lock Risk
threats.hypothesis_description: Users who call requestWithdrawal() transfer their LRON shares to the vault contract. There is no mechanism to cancel a withdrawal request and reclaim shares. If finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() is never called for the current epoch (due to operator unavailability, the onlyOperator bug INV-ACCESS-001, or contract pause), user shares are permanently locked in the vault with no recovery path. Combined with the onlyOperator bug, if the owner is also flagged as operator, ALL withdrawal requests become permanently irrecoverable.
threats.investigation_rationale: requestWithdrawal transfers shares via _transfer(msg.sender, address(this), _shares). The only path to recover value is through finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch (burns shares, sends WRON to escrow) followed by redeem(epoch). There is no cancelWithdrawal function to return shares to the user.

proof_of_signal.pattern: shares transferred to vault via _transfer with no cancel/recovery mechanism; finalization is sole exit path

preconditions: User calls requestWithdrawal with non-zero shares
finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch is not called for the current epoch

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: One-way transfer with no cancellation mechanism
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: User loses control of shares with only operator-dependent recovery
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cancelWithdrawal function; no timeout-based auto-return

analysis_questions: Is there any alternate path for the vault to return locked shares?
Could the owner use a different function to process locked shares?
What is the maximum duration funds could be locked?
validation_requirements: Confirm no alternate recovery path exists in the codebase
Test scenario: requestWithdrawal followed by indefinite finalization delay
Assess interaction with onlyOperator bug

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users request withdrawal during normal operation. The operator (or owner) becomes unavailable or the onlyOperator bug prevents finalization. All shares in the current epoch's locked pool are permanently inaccessible. Users cannot withdraw, redeem, or transfer those shares.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all shares submitted via requestWithdrawal for the unfinalized epoch
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who called requestWithdrawal for the affected epoch

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for withdrawal cancellation or recovery from failed finalization

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Locked shares visible on-chain in vault balance

threats.title: redeem(uint256 _epoch) Emits Wrong Epoch in WithdrawalClaimed Event
threats.hypothesis_description: In the redeem(uint256 _epoch) function, a local variable `uint256 epoch = withdrawalEpoch` shadows the parameter _epoch. The WithdrawalClaimed event emits `epoch` (current global withdrawalEpoch) instead of `_epoch` (the actual epoch being redeemed). This causes all event emissions to reference the current epoch rather than the claimed epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: Line `uint256 epoch = withdrawalEpoch;` creates a local variable used in the event. The event should reference _epoch. This is a clear variable naming bug.

proof_of_signal.pattern: emit WithdrawalClaimed(msg.sender, epoch, shares, assets) — epoch is withdrawalEpoch, not _epoch

preconditions: User calls redeem for a finalized epoch
withdrawalEpoch has advanced past the epoch being redeemed

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Local variable shadows function parameter in event emission
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Event data is incorrect — off-chain indexers track wrong epoch
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No compiler warning on unused _epoch in event context

analysis_questions: Do any off-chain systems depend on the epoch field in WithdrawalClaimed?
Could this cause accounting errors in protocol analytics?
validation_requirements: Verify event emission with a test: redeem epoch 0 when withdrawalEpoch is 5

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User redeems epoch 2 when current withdrawalEpoch is 5. Event emits epoch 5 instead of epoch 2. Off-chain indexers incorrectly attribute the claim to epoch 5.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Off-chain systems, dashboards, analytics relying on event data

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying correct epoch in WithdrawalClaimed event

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare event epoch vs function parameter in transaction traces

threats.title: Permissionless pruneValidatorList Can Deflate totalAssets During Transient States
threats.hypothesis_description: pruneValidatorList() is callable by any address and removes validators that show zero rewards and zero staking across all proxies. If the Ronin staking contract has transient states (e.g., after undelegation but before cooldown completion, or during block-level state transitions), a validator could temporarily show 0 for staking amounts despite having active stake. Pruning during this window removes the validator from the tracking list, causing getTotalStaked() and getTotalRewards() to exclude their stake/rewards from totalAssets(), deflating share price.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function queries point-in-time values from the external staking contract. If these values are temporarily zero (due to staking contract internal state transitions), the validator is incorrectly pruned. The pruned validator's stake is no longer counted in totalAssets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control; point-in-time staking checks may miss transient non-zero states

preconditions: Validator has active stake but Ronin staking contract returns 0 during transient state
Attacker calls pruneValidatorList during this window

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Permissionless function with external state dependency
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Staking contract may have transient zero-balance states during undelegation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No access control, no cooldown, no operator confirmation

analysis_questions: Does Ronin staking contract have cooldown periods where staking amounts read as 0?
Can undelegation create a window where getManyStakingAmounts returns 0?
What is the share price impact of pruning an active validator?
validation_requirements: Test pruneValidatorList timing relative to undelegation
Query Ronin staking contract during various state transitions
Measure totalAssets before and after incorrect pruning

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker observes an undelegateAmount call on-chain. In a subsequent block, they call pruneValidatorList. If the staking contract shows 0 for the undelegated validator (cooldown in progress), the validator is pruned. totalAssets drops by the staked amount. Attacker deposits at deflated price, waits for validator to be re-added via next delegation, redeems at restored price.
prerequisites: Ronin staking has transient zero-balance states
Validator has active but temporarily invisible stake

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Share price manipulation proportional to pruned validator's stake
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders experience temporary share price deflation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Validator re-added on next delegation; temporary loss for those who withdrew

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for pruning during transient staking states

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Capital for deposit at deflated price
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block: observe undelegation, wait for transient state, prune, deposit

threats.title: LiquidProxy Balance-Based Harvest Accounting Inflates Fees via External RON Donations
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.harvest() uses address(this).balance after claimRewards as the total claimed amount. The proxy has an open receive() function allowing anyone to send native RON. Any pre-existing native RON (from external sends, prior incomplete operations, or donations) is counted as harvested rewards. This inflates operatorFeeAmount (fee charged on donated RON) and distorts accounting. The donated RON enters the vault as WRON, but the fee is charged on the full amount including the non-reward donation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function should track balance delta (balance after claims minus balance before) rather than absolute balance. The open receive() allows anyone to deposit RON into the proxy pre-harvest.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 claimedAmount = address(this).balance; — no delta tracking, open receive()

preconditions: Native RON exists in proxy prior to harvest (via external send or prior residual)
harvest() is called

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LiquidProxy.sol lines 33-35 and receive() at line 92
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Absolute balance used instead of balance delta; open receive()
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Pre-existing RON counted as rewards, inflating operatorFeeAmount
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance snapshot before claims; no access control on receive()

analysis_questions: Can RON accumulate in the proxy from prior undelegation operations?
Is there a profitable attack from donating RON to inflate fees?
Does the donated RON ultimately benefit or harm shareholders?
validation_requirements: Send RON to proxy, call harvest, verify claimed amount includes donation
Calculate net impact on totalAssets and operatorFeeAmount
Verify whether shareholders or fee recipient benefits

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker sends X RON to a LiquidProxy. On next harvest, claimedAmount = actual_rewards + X. operatorFeeAmount increases by (actual_rewards + X) * fee / BIPS. The extra fee on X comes from depositor funds when fetchOperatorFee is called. While X RON enters the vault as WRON, the fee on X is extracted from existing depositors.
prerequisites: Attacker can send RON to proxy address
Harvest is called after the donation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Fee overcharge proportional to donated amount * operatorFee / BIPS — griefing attack on depositors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors pay the inflated fee

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for harvest with pre-existing proxy balance

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Donation amount (attacker loses most of it)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Donation before harvest in separate transactions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare claimed amount with actual staking rewards

threats.title: ERC4626 First Depositor Inflation Attack Due to Zero _decimalsOffset
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidRon does not override _decimalsOffset() (defaults to 0), providing minimal virtual share/asset offset (10^0 = 1). An attacker can be the first depositor with 1 wei, mint 1 share, then donate a large WRON amount directly to the vault. This inflates totalAssets so that subsequent depositors' previewDeposit returns 0 shares for substantial deposits. The attacker then redeems their 1 share for the entire vault balance including the victim's deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: OpenZeppelin's ERC4626 uses _decimalsOffset to create virtual shares/assets that resist inflation. With offset=0, the virtual shares are just +1, providing negligible protection. The classic first-depositor attack becomes viable.
deployment_assumptions: Deployment script deposits 10000 ether as first deposit, effectively mitigating this attack in the expected deployment
threats.config_evidence: LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol: liquidRon.deposit{value: 10000 ether}() — initial large deposit provides protection. However, the code itself has no minimum deposit or dead shares mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No _decimalsOffset override; inherits default 0 from OpenZeppelin ERC4626

preconditions: Vault has zero or near-zero total supply (no initial seed deposit)
Attacker is the first depositor
WRON can be transferred directly to the vault address

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LiquidRon.sol — no _decimalsOffset override visible in contract
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: 1-share inflation attack feasible with ~10 WRON donation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum deposit, no dead shares, no _decimalsOffset override

analysis_questions: Does the deployment always include a large initial deposit?
Could the vault reach zero supply after deployment (all shares redeemed)?
What is the minimum donation needed to steal next deposit?
validation_requirements: Verify OpenZeppelin version confirms _decimalsOffset defaults to 0
Simulate: deposit 1 wei, donate 10 WRON, check next depositor's shares
Assess if vault can reach zero supply in production

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If deployed without initial seed deposit, or if all shares are later redeemed: attacker deposits 1 wei (1 share), transfers 10 WRON to vault, victim deposits 9.99 WRON → gets 0 shares. Attacker redeems 1 share for ~20 WRON.
prerequisites: Attacker is first depositor or total supply returns to ~0

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Near-total theft of subsequent depositor's funds

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for first depositor attack scenario

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Small WRON donation (~10 WRON to steal ~10 WRON from victim)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Must be first depositor or when total supply is near zero
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large donation to vault visible on-chain

threats.title: undelegateAmount Assumes Immediate RON Return From bulkUndelegate
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.undelegateAmount() calculates totalUndelegated from input amounts, calls bulkUndelegate, then immediately wraps and sends totalUndelegated worth of RON to the vault via _depositRONTo. If the Ronin staking contract has a cooldown period or does not immediately release native RON upon undelegation, the proxy won't have sufficient balance and _depositRONTo will revert (WRON deposit requires msg.value). This would make undelegation permanently impossible.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function assumes bulkUndelegate synchronously returns native RON to the proxy. If the Ronin staking protocol has a waiting period (common in PoS staking), the RON won't be available immediately. Additionally, if slashing reduces the actual returned amount below totalUndelegated, the function reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _depositRONTo(vault, totalUndelegated) called immediately after bulkUndelegate without checking actual received balance

preconditions: Ronin staking contract has a cooldown period for undelegation
OR slashing reduces actual returned amount below requested

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Assumed immediate RON return; no actual balance check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _depositRONTo requires msg.value worth of native RON which may not be available
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance check between bulkUndelegate and _depositRONTo

analysis_questions: Does Ronin staking's bulkUndelegate return RON immediately or have a cooldown?
Can slashing reduce the returned amount?
Would the function revert silently or with a specific error?
validation_requirements: Review Ronin staking contract for undelegation mechanics
Test undelegateAmount with Ronin staking that has cooldown
Verify WRON.deposit behavior when contract has insufficient native balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Operator calls undelegateAmount to free up liquidity for user withdrawals. bulkUndelegate enters a cooldown period. _depositRONTo fails because the proxy has no native RON yet. The transaction reverts. The operator cannot undelegate funds, and user withdrawal requests cannot be fulfilled.
prerequisites: Ronin staking has undelegation cooldown or slashing

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Funds permanently locked in staking if undelegation is always delayed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users waiting for undelegated funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May need protocol upgrade or manual intervention

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for delayed undelegation or partial return scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Depends on Ronin staking cooldown period

threats.title: _checkUserCanReceiveRon Uses Zero-Value Check That Doesn't Guarantee Non-Zero Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: _checkUserCanReceiveRon sends a 0-value call to verify the user can receive RON. However, many contracts accept 0-value calls (2300 gas stipend sufficient) but revert on non-zero value transfers (e.g., due to gas limits in receive/fallback or conditional logic). A user contract could pass this check during requestWithdrawal but later fail to receive RON in the redeem(epoch) call, permanently locking their withdrawal claim — the request is marked fulfilled but the assets can never be sent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The check `payable(_user).call{value: 0}('')` succeeds for almost all addresses but doesn't validate ability to receive actual value. When redeem(epoch) calls _withdrawRONTo which sends non-zero RON, the call may fail with ErrWithdrawFailed, but request.fulfilled is NOT yet set (wait — checking the code: fulfilled IS set before the transfer). Actually: request.fulfilled = true is set BEFORE _withdrawRONTo. So if _withdrawRONTo reverts, the entire transaction reverts and fulfilled stays false. User can retry. But if the user contract never accepts non-zero RON, claims are permanently stuck.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payable(_user).call{value: 0}('') — 0-value check doesn't prove non-zero value acceptance

preconditions: User is a contract that accepts 0-value calls but reverts on non-zero value
User calls requestWithdrawal (passes check)
User later calls redeem (reverts on _withdrawRONTo)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: 0-value call check is insufficient to validate non-zero RON receipt
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Contract users may pass check but fail actual withdrawal
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum value check; no WRON fallback option

analysis_questions: Are there common contract patterns that accept 0-value but reject non-zero?
Does the revert in _withdrawRONTo properly roll back fulfilled status?
Is there an alternative path for contract users to claim WRON instead of RON?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract that accepts 0-value but reverts on non-zero, test requestWithdrawal + redeem flow
Verify transaction reverts properly (no partial state changes)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A smart contract wallet passes _checkUserCanReceiveRon during requestWithdrawal. Shares are locked in the epoch. After finalization, the contract calls redeem(epoch). _withdrawRONTo sends non-zero RON which the contract cannot accept. The call reverts. The user's shares remain locked — they can keep retrying, but if the contract can never accept RON, the funds are permanently stuck.
prerequisites: User is a contract with restricted receive/fallback

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: User's withdrawal claim is permanently unredeemable if they can never accept native RON
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Contract-based users with restricted receive functionality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None if the user contract cannot be upgraded

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test with contracts that accept 0-value but reject non-zero value

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: 2300 gas stipend vs actual transfer gas requirements
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Repeated failed redeem transactions from specific addresses
